title: 132-Million-Year-Old Dinosaur Tracks Found on a South African Beach Rewrite History
summary:
Picture this: you're a scientist walking along a rocky beach on the coast of South Africa, looking for dinosaur teeth. Instead, you spot something far more incredible — footprints that are 132 million years old.

That's exactly what happened to a team of researchers near Knysna, in South Africa's Western Cape. They were exploring a tiny outcrop of rock called the Brenton Formation — the only nearby exposure of rock from the early Cretaceous Period (145 million to 66 million years ago). Most of it is submerged at high tide. The team hoped to find a theropod dinosaur tooth, like one discovered there by a 13-year-old boy in 2017. But then Linda Helm spotted something else: dinosaur tracks. A closer look revealed more than two dozen possible footprints.

These tracks are rewriting history. They are the youngest dinosaur tracks ever found in southern Africa — 50 million years younger than the youngest tracks from the famous Karoo Basin. For decades, scientists believed that after massive volcanic eruptions covered the region with lava about 182 million years ago, dinosaurs largely disappeared from southern Africa. These tracks prove that dinosaurs continued to live and thrive there for millions of years after those dramatic events.

The site itself is remarkable. The Brenton Formation exposure is tiny — no more than 40 meters long and five meters wide (about the size of a tennis court). Finding dozens of tracks in such a limited area suggests that dinosaurs were fairly common in this region during the Cretaceous Period. Some tracks are preserved on flat rock surfaces, while others appear in cross-section within the cliffs.

These tracks represent only the second known set of Cretaceous dinosaur tracks in South Africa. They join a sparse fossil record from the Western Cape, which previously included only a few isolated sauropod teeth, scattered bones, and the theropod tooth found by the 13-year-old.

The tracks lie in the modern intertidal zone, meaning they're covered by seawater at high tide twice a day. So every discovery is a race against the clock. But for the scientists working there, it's worth it. Each footprint is a message from a time when dinosaurs roamed a very different world — and it's a message that's still being decoded.
why_it_matters: This rewrites the timeline of dinosaur existence in southern Africa, proving they lived there longer than assumed, and highlights the urgency of studying coastal fossil sites threatened by erosion.

keywords.explanation: A small rock exposure on the South African coast where the tracks were found.
A time period from 145 million to 66 million years ago when dinosaurs still lived.
A tooth from a meat-eating dinosaur, found at the same site in 2017.
A region in South Africa with many older dinosaur fossils.
The area of the beach that is underwater at high tide and exposed at low tide.
The scientist who first noticed the dinosaur tracks.

How big is the exposure where the tracks were found?
options: no more than 40 meters long and 5 meters wide
questions.correct_answer: no more than 40 meters long and 5 meters wide

questions.question: What did a 13-year-old boy discover at the site in 2017?
How much younger are these tracks compared to the youngest tracks from the Karoo Basin?
What zone are the tracks located in today?
How many sets of Cretaceous dinosaur tracks had been found in South Africa before this?

background_read: The early Cretaceous Period (145-100 million years ago) was a time when dinosaurs diversified.
In southern Africa, massive volcanic eruptions around 182 million years ago covered the Karoo Basin with lava, leading many scientists to believe dinosaurs disappeared from the region.
However, recent discoveries like these tracks show ongoing presence.
The Brenton Formation is a small marine sedimentary rock exposure on the South African coast.
Finding tracks in such a high-energy coastal environment is rare because they are often eroded by wave action.
The only other Cretaceous dinosaur tracks in South Africa were found in the Kirkwood Formation.
Article_Structure: LEAD: A team of scientists was looking for dinosaur teeth on a South African beach.
└─ Instead, they found something far more exciting: dinosaur tracks.
KEY EVENT: Linda Helm spotted more than two dozen footprints in the Brenton Formation near Knysna.
├─ WHERE: A tiny rock exposure only 40m long and 5m wide, in the intertidal zone.
├─ AGE: 132 million years old, early Cretaceous Period.
└─ SIGNIFICANCE: They are the youngest dinosaur tracks ever found in southern Africa, 50 million years younger than those in the Karoo Basin.
CONTEXT: Scientists previously thought dinosaurs disappeared from the region after huge volcanic eruptions 182 million years ago.
└─ These tracks prove they survived and thrived.
EVIDENCE: Some tracks are preserved on flat rock surfaces, others in cross-section in cliffs.
└─ Previous finds at the site include a theropod tooth discovered by a 13-year-old in 2017.
OPEN QUESTION: Only the second set of Cretaceous tracks in South Africa; what else is hidden in the remaining rock?

perspectives.description: This fills a 50-million-year gap in the fossil record and shows dinosaurs thrived after volcanic upheavals. It makes southern Africa a new focus for Cretaceous dinosaur research.
The site is in the intertidal zone, constantly eroding. Without protective measures, the tracks may vanish before they are fully studied.

The Brenton Formation is a rare marine sedimentary deposit from the Cretaceous; finding dinosaur tracks in such a context is unusual. It suggests that the dinosaurs lived near the coast and that the environment was different from today.
With the success at this small exposure, there is incentive to search more of the coastline for similar tracks. New funding and technology, like 3D scanning, could document the site rapidly.
